enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted web application for streaming your own music and video library from any device.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ampache is a free, open-source AGPL-3.0 web app for streaming and managing your personal music and video collection.

features.title: Web-based streaming
features.description: Stream your personal music and video library from a browser or the built-in HTML5 web player.

features.title: Catalog management
features.description: Browse, organize, and synchronize your media catalog with metadata pulled from embedded tags and filenames.

features.title: Multi-client support
features.description: Connect from phones, tablets, and televisions using a range of compatible third-party client apps.

features.title: API access
features.description: Documented API with examples lets developers build custom clients and integrations.

features.title: Localplay control
features.description: Control playback on remote audio output devices directly from the Ampache interface.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Officially supported Docker images make self-hosting straightforward.

pros: Completely free and open source under AGPL-3.0
Actively maintained project with a long history dating back to 2001
Supports both music and video streaming from a single install
Broad third-party client app ecosystem
Full control over your data via self-hosting
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic server administration skills
No official managed/cloud hosting option
Web interface design is less polished than commercial streaming apps
Setup and catalog configuration can be technical for non-developers

faqs.question: Is Ampache free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampache is completely free and open source, licensed under AGPL-3.0.

faqs.question: Do I need to host Ampache myself?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampache is a self-hosted application; you deploy it on your own server or via Docker.

faqs.question: Can Ampache stream video as well as music?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampache is a combined audio/video streaming application and file manager.

faqs.question: What mobile or desktop apps work with Ampache?
faqs.answer: Ampache supports a range of third-party client applications for phones, tablets, and televisions that connect via its API.

use_cases.title: Personal music streaming server
use_cases.description: Host your own music library and stream it from anywhere without relying on a commercial service.

use_cases.title: Family media server
use_cases.description: Share a household's combined music and video collection across multiple devices and users.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Ampache?
paragraphs: Ampache is a free and open-source web application for streaming and managing personal audio and video collections. First released in 2001, it lets users access their own media library from a browser or a range of compatible client apps on phones, tablets, and televisions.

The project is licensed under AGPL-3.0 and is deployed by self-hosting on a personal server or via Docker, giving users full control over their catalog and data rather than relying on a third-party streaming service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Ampache is for
paragraphs: Ampache suits self-hosters and home lab users who already have a personal music or video collection and want a self-controlled way to stream it to their own devices, rather than uploading their media to a commercial cloud platform.

enrichment.short_description: Decentralized, federated open-source platform for self-hosting and sharing music and podcasts across independent servers ('pods').
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Funkwhale is a free, open-source federated audio platform for self-hosting your own music and podcast server, or 'pod'.

features.title: Self-hosted pods
features.description: Run your own independent Funkwhale instance, called a pod, to host and stream your personal audio collection.

features.title: Federation across servers
features.description: Pods federate with one another so users on different servers can discover and share content without a central authority.

features.title: Podcast support
features.description: Subscribe to and manage podcasts alongside your music library within the same platform.

features.title: Playlist and library sharing
features.description: Share playlists, albums, or portions of your library with other users or the public.

features.title: Channels
features.description: Create channels for original music or podcasts and notify followers of new releases.

features.title: Subsonic-compatible API
features.description: Connect existing Subsonic-compatible apps and podcast clients to a Funkwhale pod.

features.title: No tracking or ads
features.description: The platform states it uses no third-party analytics, tracking, or advertising.

pros: Free, open-source, and community-developed
Decentralized/federated architecture avoids single-company control
No ads or third-party tracking
Compatible with existing Subsonic clients and podcast apps
Supports both music and podcasts in one platform
cons: Self-hosting a pod requires system administration skills
No official first-party managed hosting; paid hosting is offered only by third parties
Federation and moderation depend on individual pod administrators

faqs.question: What is a Funkwhale 'pod'?
faqs.answer: A pod is an individual, self-hosted instance of Funkwhale. Pods can federate with each other so users can discover and share content across servers.

faqs.question: Is Funkwhale free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Funkwhale is free, open-source software. Third-party providers such as Weingärtner IT and Cloud68 offer paid hosted pods if you don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: Does Funkwhale support podcasts?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to music, Funkwhale supports podcast subscriptions and playback.

faqs.question: Can I use existing apps with Funkwhale?
faqs.answer: Yes, Funkwhale works with Subsonic-compatible apps and podcast apps via its API.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted music community
use_cases.description: Run a pod for a group of friends or a community to share and discover music together.

use_cases.title: Independent artist distribution
use_cases.description: Use channels to publish original music or podcast episodes directly to followers without a centralized platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Funkwhale?
paragraphs: Funkwhale is a free, open-source platform for hosting and sharing music and podcasts. Rather than a single centralized service, Funkwhale is deployed as independent, self-hosted instances called pods, which can federate with one another so users on different pods can share and discover content.
The platform emphasizes no third-party analytics, no tracking, and no ads, and is compatible with existing Subsonic clients and podcast apps through its API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Funkwhale is for
paragraphs: Funkwhale is aimed at self-hosters and communities who want an independent, ad-free alternative to centralized streaming services and are comfortable running and administering their own server, or who use a third-party hosted pod.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted personal music streaming server with native iOS/Android apps and an optional paid Plus tier.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koel is a self-hosted, open-source music streaming server (MIT license) with a free Community edition and paid Koel Plus tier.

features.title: Lossless streaming
features.description: Stream your music collection with transparent, lossless audio support.

features.title: Cross-device sync
features.description: Keep playback and favorites synchronized as you move between devices.

features.title: Smart playlists and favorites
features.description: Build dynamic smart playlists and mark tracks as favorites.

features.title: Metadata integrations
features.description: Pull additional artist and track information from MusicBrainz, Wikipedia, Last.fm, Spotify, and YouTube.

features.title: Subsonic/OpenSubsonic API
features.description: Connect existing Subsonic and OpenSubsonic-compatible apps to a Koel server.

features.title: Native mobile apps
features.description: Dedicated iOS and Android apps are available via the App Store and Google Play.

features.title: Koel Plus
features.description: Paid tier adds multi-user support with individual libraries, extended storage drivers (S3, Nextcloud, ownCloud, Dropbox), collaboration, and white-labeling.

pros: Community edition is free and open source under the MIT license
Koel Plus is a one-time purchase, not a subscription
Native iOS and Android apps included
Compatible with Subsonic and OpenSubsonic clients
Rich metadata integrations (MusicBrainz, Last.fm, Spotify, YouTube)
cons: Multi-user support and extended storage backends require the paid Plus tier
Requires self-hosting and server administration
No official managed/cloud hosting

faqs.question: Is Koel free?
faqs.answer: The Community version of Koel is free and open source (MIT license) and will always remain so.

faqs.question: What does Koel Plus cost?
faqs.answer: Koel Plus is a pay-what-you-want, one-time purchase starting at €19.99, with no subscription or recurring fees.

faqs.question: What extra features does Koel Plus add?
faqs.answer: Koel Plus adds multi-user support with individual libraries, extended storage drivers like S3 and Nextcloud, collaboration features, and white-labeling.

faqs.question: Does Koel have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koel has native apps for iOS (App Store) and Android (Google Play).

use_cases.title: Personal music server
use_cases.description: Self-host your own music collection and stream it to any device with lossless audio support.

use_cases.title: Multi-user household streaming
use_cases.description: Use Koel Plus to give multiple users their own libraries on a shared server.

features: Extended storage drivers (S3, Nextcloud, ownCloud, Dropbox)

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Koel?
paragraphs: Koel is a self-hosted, open-source music streaming server licensed under the MIT license. Its free Community edition provides lossless streaming, smart playlists, and Subsonic/OpenSubsonic API compatibility, along with native iOS and Android apps.

Koel Plus is an optional, pay-what-you-want one-time purchase starting at €19.99 that unlocks multi-user support with individual libraries, extended storage backends such as S3 and Nextcloud, collaboration, and white-labeling — with no subscription required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Koel is for
paragraphs: Koel fits self-hosters who want a modern, lossless music streaming experience for their own library, and households or small teams that need multi-user support without committing to a recurring subscription.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source self-hosted server for organizing and streaming your personal audiobook and podcast collection.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Audiobookshelf is a free, open-source (GPL-3.0) self-hosted server for streaming your own audiobooks and podcasts.

features.title: Audiobook streaming
features.description: Stream audiobooks across multiple formats with on-the-fly conversion for playback.

features.title: Podcast management
features.description: Discover, subscribe to, and automatically download new podcast episodes.

features.title: Multi-user accounts
features.description: Create multiple user accounts with customizable permissions and per-user listening progress sync.

features.title: Metadata and cover art fetching
features.description: Automatically fetch metadata and cover art for audiobooks from external sources, with chapter editing support.

features.title: Basic ebook support
features.description: Serve and read ebooks in EPUB, PDF, CBR, and CBZ formats alongside audio content.

features.title: Progressive Web App
features.description: Access the library as a PWA with Chromecast support for casting playback to TVs.

features.title: RSS feed generation
features.description: Generate RSS feeds for audiobooks and podcasts for use in third-party apps.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL-3.0
Native iOS and Android apps in addition to the web/PWA interface
Handles both audiobooks and podcasts in one server
Active project with a large GitHub community (13.6k+ stars)
Automated metadata fetching and daily backups
cons: Requires self-hosting and Docker/server setup
iOS app has historically been limited by TestFlight beta capacity
No official managed/cloud hosting option
Ebook support is described as basic rather than full-featured

faqs.question: Is Audiobookshelf free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Audiobookshelf is free and open source, licensed under GPL-3.0.

faqs.question: Does Audiobookshelf support podcasts as well as audiobooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, it manages both audiobook and podcast libraries, including automatic podcast episode downloads.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Audiobookshelf has native iOS and Android apps, plus a Progressive Web App with Chromecast support.

faqs.question: How do I install Audiobookshelf?
faqs.answer: Audiobookshelf is self-hosted and officially supports Docker deployment.

use_cases.title: Personal audiobook library
use_cases.description: Self-host and stream a personal collection of audiobooks with progress tracking across devices.

use_cases.title: Podcast archiving and playback
use_cases.description: Subscribe to and automatically download podcasts to a personal, ad-free server.
use_cases.audience: Podcast listeners who prefer self-hosted tools

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Audiobookshelf?
paragraphs: Audiobookshelf is a free, open-source self-hosted server for organizing and streaming personal audiobook and podcast collections, licensed under GPL-3.0 and built on Node.js.

It supports multi-user accounts with progress syncing, automatic metadata and cover art fetching, podcast subscriptions with automated downloads, and basic ebook support for EPUB, PDF, CBR, and CBZ files. Native iOS and Android apps complement the web-based Progressive Web App.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Audiobookshelf is for
paragraphs: Audiobookshelf is built for self-hosters who own audiobook and podcast files and want a private, ad-free way to stream and track progress across devices without relying on a commercial subscription service.

enrichment.short_description: Free, federated video hosting platform developed by Framasoft, using peer-to-peer delivery and ActivityPub federation.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PeerTube is a free, open-source (AGPL-3.0) decentralized video platform developed by Framasoft, using P2P and ActivityPub federation.

features.title: Federated video hosting
features.description: Independent PeerTube instances federate via ActivityPub, connecting into one larger network.

features.title: Peer-to-peer delivery
features.description: Uses WebRTC-based P2P video broadcasting in the browser to reduce server bandwidth load.

features.title: Live streaming
features.description: Supports live streaming via RTMP along with built-in video editing capabilities.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Official apps are available for iOS, Android, and F-Droid for discovering and managing content.

features.title: No ads or tracking
features.description: The platform states it carries no advertising and does not track users.

features.title: Algorithm-free browsing
features.description: Content discovery avoids recommendation algorithms, with transparent, auditable open-source code.

pros: Free and open source under AGPL-3.0
No cost to install and run on your own server
Federated network already spans roughly 1,600 platforms and 1 million videos
Developed by Framasoft, a non-profit funded mostly by donations
P2P delivery reduces bandwidth costs for instance operators
cons: Requires self-hosting and server administration to run an instance
No official first-party managed/cloud hosting
Content discovery and moderation depend on which instance(s) you join or federate with

faqs.question: Is PeerTube free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PeerTube is free and open source; there is no cost to install it on your own server.

faqs.question: Who develops PeerTube?
faqs.answer: PeerTube is developed by Framasoft, a French non-profit organization funded primarily by donations.

faqs.question: What is instance federation?
faqs.answer: PeerTube instances can federate with one another via ActivityPub, letting videos and channels be followed and discovered across independently run servers.

faqs.question: Does PeerTube support live streaming?
faqs.answer: Yes, PeerTube supports live streaming via RTMP.

use_cases.title: Independent video platform
use_cases.description: Organizations or communities run their own PeerTube instance instead of relying on centralized video platforms.

use_cases.title: Creator channel with federation
use_cases.description: Creators publish videos on an instance and reach viewers across the federated PeerTube network via ActivityPub.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is PeerTube?
paragraphs: PeerTube is a free, open-source, decentralized video platform developed by Framasoft, a French non-profit, as an alternative to centralized services like YouTube, Dailymotion, or Vimeo. It is licensed under AGPL-3.0.

Independent PeerTube instances federate with one another through ActivityPub, forming a larger interconnected network — currently around 1,600 platforms hosting roughly 1 million videos — while using WebRTC-based peer-to-peer delivery to reduce server load.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who PeerTube is for
paragraphs: PeerTube suits organizations, communities, and creators who want to host and share video content without relying on centralized, ad-driven platforms, and who are willing to run or join a federated instance.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted live video streaming and chat server that works with existing broadcasting software.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Owncast is a free, open-source (MIT) self-hosted live streaming and chat server compatible with OBS and other broadcasting tools.

features.title: Self-hosted live streaming
features.description: Run your own live video streaming server with full ownership over your content and community.

features.title: Built-in chat
features.description: Includes real-time viewer chat with support for custom emotes and chatbots.

features.title: Broadcasting software compatibility
features.description: Works with existing RTMP-based tools such as OBS, Streamlabs, and Restream by pointing them at your Owncast server.

features.title: Fediverse integration
features.description: Streams can be discovered and followed from Mastodon and other Fediverse services.

features.title: Web-based player and admin
features.description: Includes a web player for viewers and a web-based admin interface for managing the stream.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Works with popular existing broadcasting software like OBS
Fediverse integration extends reach beyond the streamer's own site
Strong GitHub community with 11.4k+ stars
No platform ads or algorithmic content control
cons: Requires self-hosting and Docker/server setup
Single-user/single-stream design rather than a multi-channel platform
No official managed/cloud hosting option

faqs.question: Is Owncast free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Owncast is free and open source, licensed under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What broadcasting software works with Owncast?
faqs.answer: Owncast works with existing RTMP-based broadcasting tools such as OBS, Streamlabs, and Restream.

faqs.question: Does Owncast include chat?
faqs.answer: Yes, Owncast has built-in chat with support for custom emotes and chatbots.

faqs.question: Can Owncast streams reach Mastodon users?
faqs.answer: Yes, Owncast integrates with the Fediverse so streams can be discovered from Mastodon and similar services.

use_cases.title: Independent live streaming
use_cases.description: Stream live video from your own server instead of a centralized platform, using tools you already use like OBS.

use_cases.title: Community-owned streaming with chat
use_cases.description: Host live streams with built-in chat for a community without relying on third-party platform moderation or algorithms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Owncast?
paragraphs: Owncast is a free and open-source, self-hosted live video streaming and web chat server, licensed under the MIT license. It is designed to work with existing broadcasting software — users point tools like OBS at their Owncast server instead of a centralized platform.
The server includes a built-in chat with custom emotes and chatbot support, plus Fediverse integration so streams can be discovered and followed from Mastodon and other Fediverse services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Owncast is for
paragraphs: Owncast is for independent streamers and self-hosters who want full ownership of their live streaming and chat experience, using broadcasting tools they already have, without going through a centralized commercial streaming platform.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted video and media CMS with adaptive streaming, role-based access, and optional paid managed services.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MediaCMS is a free, open-source (AGPL-3.0) video and media CMS with paid deployment, training, and managed-service options.

features.title: Multi-format media support
features.description: Handles video, audio, PDF, and image files within a single media management platform.

features.title: Adaptive video streaming
features.description: Delivers video via HLS adaptive streaming with an intuitive player, captions, and chapters.

features.title: Automatic transcription
features.description: Includes integrated Whisper-based automatic transcription for video and audio content.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Manage users, categories, and groups with custom permissions and public, unlisted, or private workflows.

features.title: Video trimming and chapters
features.description: Built-in tools for trimming video and creating chapter markers directly in the platform.

features.title: LTI and Moodle integration
features.description: Supports LTI 1.3 and a Moodle plugin for use in educational settings.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Provides a documented REST API with Swagger documentation for integrations.

pros: Core platform is free and open source under AGPL-3.0
Self-hosted with full control over data and infrastructure
Supports multiple media types (video, audio, image, PDF) in one CMS
Built-in automatic transcription and adaptive streaming
Used by universities and organizations like Critical Commons
cons: Advanced deployment, training, and ongoing managed services require paid plans
Self-hosting requires Docker/server administration
Managed services pricing ($2,000/month) may be significant for small teams

faqs.question: Is MediaCMS free?
faqs.answer: The core MediaCMS software is free and open source under AGPL-3.0. Paid deployment, training, and managed services are available separately.

faqs.question: What does MediaCMS's managed service cost?
faqs.answer: MediaCMS offers one-time deployment and training for $2,000 and ongoing managed services for $2,000/month, per the official site.

faqs.question: Can MediaCMS handle files besides video?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaCMS supports video, audio, image, and PDF files.

faqs.question: Does MediaCMS support automatic captions?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes integrated Whisper-based automatic transcription.

use_cases.title: University or education media portal
use_cases.description: Host lecture videos and course media with LTI 1.3 and Moodle integration for learning platforms.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted video/media portal
use_cases.description: Run a private or public video and media portal with full control over hosting and access permissions.
use_cases.audience: Organizations needing a self-hosted media CMS

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MediaCMS?
paragraphs: MediaCMS is a free, open-source video and media CMS, licensed under AGPL-3.0 and built with Django, Python, Celery, and React. It supports video, audio, image, and PDF content with adaptive HLS streaming, automatic Whisper-based transcription, and role-based access control.

Beyond the free self-hosted software, MediaCMS's operators offer paid deployment and training ($2,000 one-time) and ongoing managed services ($2,000/month) for organizations that don't want to self-manage their instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MediaCMS is for
paragraphs: MediaCMS is aimed at universities, educational institutions, and organizations that need a self-hosted video and media portal with access controls, and who may optionally pay for professional deployment or managed hosting services.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source self-hosted YouTube media server for archiving, indexing, and searching personal YouTube video collections.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tube Archivist is a free, open-source (GPL-3.0) self-hosted server for archiving, indexing, and searching YouTube videos.

features.title: Channel subscriptions
features.description: Subscribe to YouTube channels and automatically track new uploads.

features.title: Video downloading
features.description: Downloads videos using yt-dlp for offline archiving.

features.title: Full-text search and indexing
features.description: Indexes video metadata to make large collections searchable.

features.title: Web playback interface
features.description: Play downloaded videos directly through a web interface, with viewed/unviewed tracking.

features.title: SponsorBlock integration
features.description: Integrates with SponsorBlock to skip sponsored segments in downloaded videos.

features.title: Jellyfin and Plex integration
features.description: Connects with Jellyfin and Plex media servers for extended playback options.

features.title: Browser extension
features.description: Firefox and Chrome-based browser extension available for managing the archive.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-3.0
Solves the problem of searching large personal YouTube archives
Integrates with existing media servers (Jellyfin, Plex)
SponsorBlock and subtitle support built in
Active project with 8.3k+ GitHub stars
cons: Requires self-hosting via Docker with meaningful RAM requirements (4GB recommended for larger installs)
No official managed/cloud hosting
Depends on yt-dlp, which can be affected by upstream YouTube changes

faqs.question: Is Tube Archivist free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tube Archivist is free and open source, licensed under GPL-3.0.

faqs.question: How does Tube Archivist download videos?
faqs.answer: It uses yt-dlp to download videos from subscribed YouTube channels.

faqs.question: Does Tube Archivist work with Plex or Jellyfin?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with both Jellyfin and Plex media servers.

faqs.question: What are the system requirements?
faqs.answer: Tube Archivist requires Docker, roughly 2GB RAM for testing setups, and 4GB or more for mid-to-large installations.

use_cases.title: Personal YouTube archive
use_cases.description: Automatically download and index subscribed YouTube channels for offline, searchable access.

use_cases.title: Media server add-on
use_cases.description: Feed a personal YouTube archive into an existing Jellyfin or Plex library.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Tube Archivist?
paragraphs: Tube Archivist is a free, open-source, self-hosted YouTube media server, licensed under GPL-3.0. It lets users subscribe to YouTube channels, download videos with yt-dlp, and index them with metadata so large collections remain searchable.

The platform includes SponsorBlock integration, subtitle and comment archiving, and can connect with Jellyfin or Plex for extended media server functionality, along with a browser extension for managing the archive.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Tube Archivist is for
paragraphs: Tube Archivist is built for self-hosters with large personal YouTube archives who need a searchable, indexed way to manage and play back downloaded videos, often alongside an existing Jellyfin or Plex setup.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted web radio management suite with a turnkey installer for the full radio software stack.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AzuraCast is a free, open-source (AGPL-3.0) self-hosted web radio management suite with built-in Icecast/Shoutcast support.

features.title: Turnkey radio stack installer
features.description: Installs and configures the full radio software stack, removing the need to set up each component manually.

features.title: Web-based station management
features.description: Manage one or more radio stations through a modern web app interface.

features.title: Icecast/Shoutcast streaming
features.description: Built-in support for Icecast and Shoutcast streaming servers.

features.title: Liquidsoap automation
features.description: Uses Liquidsoap for playlist automation and audio processing.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Officially supported via Docker with sample Docker Compose files provided.

pros: Free and open source under AGPL-3.0
Bundles the entire radio stack instead of requiring separate tools
Active project with 3.9k+ GitHub stars and a Discord community
Comprehensive official documentation for installation
Volunteer-maintained with no paid tier required for full functionality
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic Linux/server administration
No official managed/cloud hosting offering
Relies on donations for project funding rather than commercial revenue

faqs.question: Is AzuraCast free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AzuraCast is free and open source, licensed under AGPL-3.0.

faqs.question: What streaming servers does AzuraCast support?
faqs.answer: AzuraCast includes built-in support for Icecast and Shoutcast.

faqs.question: How is AzuraCast installed?
faqs.answer: AzuraCast provides a turnkey installer and full Docker support with sample Docker Compose files.

faqs.question: Does AzuraCast offer hosted/cloud radio hosting?
faqs.answer: No official managed hosting is offered; AzuraCast is designed to be self-hosted.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted internet radio station
use_cases.description: Run a complete internet radio station, including streaming and automation, on your own server.

use_cases.title: Community or campus radio
use_cases.description: Manage multiple stations from one web interface for a community, school, or organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is AzuraCast?
paragraphs: AzuraCast is a free, open-source, self-hosted web radio management suite, licensed under AGPL-3.0. It provides a turnkey installer for the full radio software stack, including Icecast/Shoutcast streaming and Liquidsoap-based automation, managed through a modern web interface.
The project is volunteer-maintained and supports Docker deployment with sample Compose files, and has an active community with over 3,900 GitHub stars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who AzuraCast is for
paragraphs: AzuraCast is built for independent broadcasters, hobbyists, and community or campus radio operators who want to self-host a complete internet radio station without stitching together separate streaming and automation tools.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source suite of WordPress plugins for self-hosted podcast publishing, playback, and subscription.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Podlove is a free, open-source suite of WordPress plugins for self-hosted podcast publishing, an HTML5 player, and a subscribe widget.

features.title: Podlove Publisher
features.description: WordPress plugin for managing podcast episodes with an interface designed to make publishing as simple as writing a blog post.

features.title: Podlove Web Player
features.description: HTML5 audio/video player optimized for podcasts, usable standalone or integrated with Publisher.

features.title: Podcast Subscribe Button
features.description: Widget that reads a podcast's RSS feed and lets listeners subscribe in their preferred podcast app.

features.title: No cloud dependency
features.description: Runs entirely on a self-hosted WordPress installation with no reliance on third-party cloud services.

pros: Free, open-source software available on GitHub
No cloud dependencies — fully self-hosted on WordPress
Modular tools (Publisher, Player, Subscribe Button) can be used independently
Used by established podcasts such as Forschergeist and Logbuch: Netzpolitik
Simplifies podcast publishing to a blog-post-like workflow
cons: Requires an existing self-hosted WordPress site
No official paid support tier or managed hosting
Documentation and community are largely oriented toward a German-speaking user base

faqs.question: Is Podlove free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Podlove's WordPress plugins are open source and free to use.

faqs.question: Does Podlove require WordPress?
faqs.answer: Yes, Podlove Publisher is built as a WordPress plugin, so it requires a self-hosted WordPress site.

faqs.question: What is the Podlove Web Player?
faqs.answer: It's an HTML5 audio/video player optimized for podcasts that can be used standalone or alongside Podlove Publisher.

faqs.question: Does Podlove depend on any cloud service?
faqs.answer: No, Podlove is designed with no cloud dependencies and runs entirely on your own WordPress hosting.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted podcast publishing
use_cases.description: Publish and manage podcast episodes directly from a self-hosted WordPress site rather than a third-party hosting platform.

use_cases.title: Embeddable podcast player
use_cases.description: Add the Podlove Web Player or Subscribe Button to any website to let visitors play or subscribe to episodes.
use_cases.audience: Podcasters embedding players on external sites

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Podlove?
paragraphs: Podlove is a free, open-source suite of WordPress plugins for self-hosted podcasting. It includes Podlove Publisher, which turns episode management into a workflow as simple as writing a blog post, the Podlove Web Player, an HTML5 player optimized for podcasts, and a Podcast Subscribe Button widget for RSS-based subscriptions.
The project emphasizes no cloud dependencies, running entirely on a self-hosted WordPress installation, and is used by podcasts including Forschergeist, Lage der Nation, and Logbuch: Netzpolitik.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Podlove is for
paragraphs: Podlove is for podcasters who already run a self-hosted WordPress site and want an independent, cloud-free way to publish, play, and let listeners subscribe to their show without relying on a third-party podcast hosting platform.
